text:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT BOMBAY
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 412 OF 2023

Pronounced on: 18th September 2023

Appellant  : Ramesh Vinayak Patil
Respondent : The State of Maharashtra

Counsel for Appellant  : Mr. Sanjay R. Deshpande
Counsel for Respondent : Ms. Priya K. Joshi (APP)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

JUDGMENT
(Per Hon'ble Justice A.M. Khanwilkar)

1. The present appeal arises from the judgment and order dated 3rd August 2023 passed by the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Pune, in Sessions Case No. 87 of 2022, whereby the appellant was convicted under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code and sentenced to imprisonment for life.

2. The prosecution case, in brief, is as follows. On the night of 14th August 2022, the deceased Suresh Patil, aged 48 years, was found murdered in his residence at Kothrud, Pune. The First Information Report (FIR) was lodged by the deceased's wife, Smt. Kavita Patil, at Kothrud Police Station at 11:45 PM on 14th August 2022. The appellant, a distant cousin of the deceased, was named as the prime suspect.

3. The investigating officer, PI Arun Shelar, took up the investigation and arrested the appellant Ramesh Vinayak Patil on 16th August 2022, i.e., two days after the murder. The arrest was effected from the appellant's residence at Wakad, Pune, pursuant to a tip-off received by the police.

4. On 19th August 2022, the appellant was produced before the learned Judicial Magistrate First Class, Pune, and was remanded to police custody for a period of five days. Three days following the arrest, on 19th August 2022, the appellant is alleged to have led police to a dry well behind Wakad village, where the murder weapon — a blood-stained wooden baton — was recovered.

5. The bail application filed on behalf of the appellant was taken up for hearing on 25th August 2022. On that date, after hearing both sides at length, the learned JMFC rejected the bail application on the grounds that the offence was grave and the accused was a flight risk. A subsequent bail application was filed before the Sessions Court on 10th September 2022, which was also rejected on 18th September 2022.

6. The charge-sheet was filed by the investigating agency on 14th November 2022, within the stipulated period. Prior to the filing of the charge-sheet, the forensic science laboratory (FSL) report was received by the investigating officer on 2nd November 2022, confirming that the blood found on the recovered baton matched the DNA profile of the deceased.

7. The Sessions Trial commenced on 15th February 2023. Twenty-three prosecution witnesses were examined between 15th February 2023 and 20th July 2023. The defence examined two witnesses. The learned Additional Sessions Judge, after appreciating the evidence, returned a finding of guilt against the appellant and, by the impugned judgment dated 3rd August 2023, convicted and sentenced him as aforesaid.

8. Having heard the learned counsel for the parties and having gone through the record and proceedings, we are of the opinion that the prosecution has proved its case beyond reasonable doubt. The chain of circumstances — the motive of property dispute, the recovery of the weapon at the instance of the accused, the FSL report, and the testimony of eyewitnesses — forms an unbroken chain pointing unerringly to the guilt of the accused.

9. For the foregoing reasons, the appeal is dismissed. The conviction and sentence recorded by the trial court are confirmed.

(Justice A.M. Khanwilkar)
(Justice S.P. Tavade)

Mumbai, 18th September 2023.

Page 1 of 1 | Certified Copy | High Court of Bombay | Nominal Roll No. 4512

IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI

W.P. (CIVIL) NO. 9841 OF 2023

Decided on: 5th October 2023

Petitioner  : Meena Sharma
Respondents : Union of India & Ors.

Mr. Vikram Nair, Senior Advocate for Petitioner
Ms. Ritu Bhatt, CGSC for Respondent No. 1

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

JUDGMENT
(Per Justice Rekha Palli)

1. This writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenges the order dated 12th July 2023 passed by the Central Administrative Tribunal, Principal Bench, New Delhi, rejecting the petitioner's application for reinstatement in service.

2. The petitioner, Smt. Meena Sharma, was employed as a Senior Clerk in the Ministry of Finance with effect from 1st March 2009. On 7th January 2023, she was served with a charge-sheet alleging misappropriation of government funds to the tune of Rs. 4.2 lakhs during the financial year 2021-22.

3. Simultaneously with the issuance of the charge-sheet on 7th January 2023, the petitioner was placed under suspension by an order of the competent authority. The suspension order was challenged before the CAT by O.A. No. 141 of 2023, filed on 20th January 2023. That application was dismissed by the Tribunal on 15th February 2023, holding the suspension order to be in conformity with the applicable service rules.

4. The departmental inquiry commenced on 3rd March 2023. The Inquiry Officer, Shri P.K. Gupta, recorded statements of six departmental witnesses between 3rd March 2023 and 25th April 2023. The petitioner was given a reasonable opportunity to cross-examine the witnesses, of which she availed herself only partially, citing ill health.

5. The Inquiry Report was submitted on 10th May 2023. The Inquiry Officer found the charges substantially proved against the petitioner. Consequent upon the receipt of the Inquiry Report, the disciplinary authority issued a show-cause notice on 20th May 2023, calling upon the petitioner to submit her response within fifteen days.

6. After considering the petitioner's reply received on 2nd June 2023, the disciplinary authority, by an order dated 28th June 2023, imposed the penalty of compulsory retirement upon the petitioner with immediate effect. Subsequently, the petitioner filed O.A. No. 987 of 2023 before the CAT on 5th July 2023, challenging the penalty order. The said OA was rejected by the impugned order dated 12th July 2023.

7. Three weeks after the impugned CAT order, the present writ petition was filed on 5th August 2023. Thereafter, an interim stay of the penalty order was granted by this Court on 10th August 2023, subject to the petitioner depositing 50% of the allegedly misappropriated amount.

8. Having heard the parties, we find that the inquiry was conducted in substantial compliance with the principles of natural justice. The petitioner was accorded adequate opportunity. The finding of the Inquiry Officer is based on cogent evidence including ledger entries, bank statements, and the testimony of witnesses. We find no jurisdictional infirmity or perversity in the impugned order of the CAT.

9. The writ petition is accordingly dismissed. The interim stay granted by this Court on 10th August 2023 stands vacated forthwith.

(Justice Rekha Palli)
(Justice Arun Kumar Tyagi)

New Delhi, 5th October 2023.

IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT MADRAS

CRL. O.P. NO. 18742 OF 2023
AND
Crl. M.P. No. 11021 of 2023

Ordered on: 22nd November 2023

Petitioner  : Arulmurugan @ Arun
Respondent  : The State rep. by the Inspector of Police, Coimbatore North PS

Mr. T. Ravichandran for Petitioner
Mr. R. Venkatesh, APP for Respondent

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ORDER
(Per Justice G. Jayachandran)

1. The petitioner has filed this petition under Section 482 Cr.P.C. seeking quashing of FIR No. 642 of 2023 registered on 5th September 2023 at Coimbatore North Police Station for offences punishable under Sections 341, 323, 324 and 506(ii) IPC.

2. According to the prosecution, on the evening of 4th September 2023, at approximately 7:30 PM, the petitioner along with two associates allegedly waylaid one Mr. Kandasamy, a petty shop owner, near the Gandhipuram bus stand, Coimbatore. He is alleged to have caused hurt to the complainant using a wooden stick and extorted a sum of Rs. 10,000/- under threat of dire consequences.

3. The complainant Kandasamy lodged a complaint on 5th September 2023 at 9:00 AM at Coimbatore North Police Station. Based on the said complaint, FIR No. 642 of 2023 was registered. The petitioner was arrested on 6th September 2023. His arrest was followed by a remand to judicial custody on the same date. The petitioner's application for bail before the learned Judicial Magistrate, Coimbatore, was filed on 8th September 2023.

4. On 12th September 2023, the learned Magistrate rejected the bail application, holding that the petitioner was involved in a similar offence registered as FIR No. 310 of 2022 at the same police station, and his release was likely to affect the witnesses. Seven days after the rejection of bail by the Magistrate, i.e., on 19th September 2023, the petitioner filed a bail application before the learned Principal Sessions Judge, Coimbatore.

5. The Sessions Court allowed the bail on 28th September 2023, subject to conditions including executing a personal bond for Rs. 25,000/- and surrender of passport. The petitioner was accordingly released on 29th September 2023. Thereafter, about three weeks following his release, on 20th October 2023, the petitioner filed the present petition before this Court seeking quashing of the FIR.

6. The respondent police filed a counter-affidavit on 10th November 2023 stating that the investigation was at an advanced stage, the petitioner's mobile phone records placed him at the scene, and CCTV footage from a nearby ATM corroborated the complainant's version.

7. Having regard to the totality of facts and circumstances, including the stage of investigation and the availability of corroborative material, this Court is not inclined to exercise its power under Section 482 Cr.P.C. at this juncture. The petition is dismissed. However, liberty is granted to the petitioner to approach the Trial Court if charges are ultimately framed, and that Court shall consider such application afresh on its own merits.

(Justice G. Jayachandran)

Chennai, 22nd November 2023.

IN THE HIGH COURT AT CALCUTTA
CIVIL REVISIONAL JURISDICTION

C.O. NO. 1234 OF 2024

Heard and Decided on: 14th March 2024

Petitioner  : Bimalendu Sarkar
Opposite Party : Smt. Chhaya Sarkar & Ors.

Mr. Dipankar Ghosh, Advocate for Petitioner
Mr. Subrata Mukherjee, Advocate for Opposite Parties

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

JUDGMENT
(Per Justice Moushumi Bhattacharya)

1. The revisional application impugns the order dated 31st January 2024 passed by the learned Civil Judge (Senior Division), Barasat, in Title Suit No. 88 of 2019, rejecting the petitioner's application under Order XXXIX Rule 1 and 2 CPC for grant of temporary injunction.

2. Title Suit No. 88 of 2019 was instituted by the petitioner on 17th June 2019, seeking declaration of title and permanent injunction in respect of a parcel of land measuring 5 kathas situated at Mouza Rajarhat, District North 24 Parganas. The petitioner claims to have purchased the said land from one Taraknath Dey by a registered sale deed dated 5th March 2015.

3. The opposite party No. 1, Smt. Chhaya Sarkar, claims title to the same property by virtue of a registered sale deed dated 11th September 2017 executed in her favour. She contends that she purchased the property in good faith for valuable consideration without notice of the petitioner's prior purchase.

4. It is the petitioner's case that he was put into possession of the property on 5th March 2015 pursuant to the sale deed. He further submits that on 3rd January 2024, the opposite parties, without any lawful authority, commenced construction on the disputed land. Upon learning of the illegal construction, the petitioner filed the injunction application on 10th January 2024, nearly three weeks after the construction began.

5. The learned trial court, by its order dated 31st January 2024, declined to grant the injunction, holding that the petitioner had not established a prima facie case of possession, and that the balance of convenience did not favour him. The court also noted that prior to the filing of the present suit in 2019, the opposite party No. 1 had filed a suit in 2018 (Title Suit No. 21 of 2018) which was pending. Accordingly, the application was dismissed.

6. Before this Court, it is argued by Mr. Ghosh that the trial court failed to appreciate that the sale deed of 2015 was executed prior in time to the sale deed of 2017, and the subsequent purchaser was not a bona fide purchaser for value without notice. He submits that the registered sale deed of 2015 was sufficient to establish prima facie title.

7. Having heard the learned counsel and perused the relevant documents, this Court finds that the learned trial court has correctly assessed the balance of convenience. The construction by the opposite parties is at an advanced stage and demolition at this stage would cause irreparable loss. Furthermore, the petitioner's delay in filing the injunction application three weeks after the commencement of construction militates against the grant of urgent equitable relief.

8. The revisional application is accordingly dismissed. The order dated 31st January 2024 passed by the learned Civil Judge (Senior Division), Barasat, is confirmed.

(Justice Moushumi Bhattacharya)

Calcultta, 14th March 2024.

Page 1 of 1 | Official Copy | High Court at Calcutta

IN THE HIGH COURT OF KERALA AT ERNAKULAM

WP (CRIMINAL) No. 1587 OF 2024

Judgment pronounced on: 30th April 2024

Petitioner  : Shajahan K.
Respondents : State of Kerala, through the Superintendent of Police, Malappuram & Ors.

Adv. K.A. Noushad for Petitioner
Public Prosecutor Adv. M. Sreejith for Respondent-State

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

JUDGMENT
(Per Justice P.B. Suresh Kumar)

1. This writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India is filed seeking a writ of habeas corpus, directing the respondents to produce the body of the petitioner's brother, Safar K., aged 32 years, who is alleged to be in illegal detention.

2. The brief facts are as follows. On 18th March 2024, Safar K. was allegedly taken into custody by a team of police officers from Malappuram Police Station in connection with an inquiry into a theft case registered as Crime No. 74 of 2024. His family members allege that from 18th March 2024, Safar has been continuously detained at an undisclosed location without being produced before any Magistrate.

3. The present writ petition was filed on 22nd March 2024, four days after the alleged unlawful detention commenced. On the same date, this Court issued notice and called for a report from the respondent-Superintendent of Police, Malappuram.

4. The respondent-State filed a counter-affidavit on 26th March 2024 denying illegal detention. It was stated that Safar K. was arrested on 18th March 2024 and was produced before the Judicial Magistrate of the First Class, Malappuram, on 19th March 2024, i.e., within 24 hours of his arrest, in compliance with Article 22(2) of the Constitution. He was thereupon remanded to judicial custody and lodged at District Jail, Malappuram, on 19th March 2024.

5. In response to the counter-affidavit, petitioner's counsel submitted on 3rd April 2024 that the respondents had not produced any remand papers to substantiate the claim of production before the Magistrate. He further submitted that on 21st March 2024, i.e., three days after the alleged arrest, the petitioner's family had personally visited the District Jail and were informed that no such person was lodged therein.

6. Pursuant to this Court's direction, the Malappuram Judicial Magistrate submitted a report on 10th April 2024 confirming that Safar K. was produced before him on 19th March 2024 and was duly remanded to judicial custody. The remand order, bearing seal and signature, was enclosed with the report. Simultaneously, the Superintendent, District Jail Malappuram, filed a compliance report on 10th April 2024, confirming that the detenu was lodged at the jail from 19th March 2024 onwards.

7. In light of the aforesaid documentary evidence establishing compliance with constitutional and legal requirements, the foundation of the writ petition stands demolished. The detention is not illegal. The detenu was arrested, produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours, and duly remanded. The grounds of habeas corpus are not made out.

8. The writ petition is accordingly dismissed. No order as to costs.

(Justice P.B. Suresh Kumar)

Ernakulam, 30th April 2024.
